articles.label: Analysis
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47M reserves question: Who decides when rainy day becomes monsoon?
articles.dek: County commissioners disagree on when to tap emergency funds. With hurricane season eight weeks out, the debate reveals deeper tensions over fiscal philosophy.
brief: Escambia County maintains $47.2 million in unassigned reserves, roughly 18% of its general fund budget
Commissioners split 3-2 last week over whether to use $8.5 million in reserves to fund drainage improvements before hurricane season
The debate exposes fundamental disagreements about fiscal policy that will shape budget negotiations through September
articles.body:
The number sits in a spreadsheet cell on page 47 of Escambia County's comprehensive annual financial report: $47,218,433. Unassigned fund balance. Money without a purpose yet.

For Commissioner Mike Kohler, that figure represents responsibility. Prudence. A buffer against the storms that sweep up from the Gulf every summer.

For Commissioner Lumon May, it represents something else entirely. Roads that flood every time it rains. Drainage systems designed for a county half this size. Problems that compound while dollars sit idle.

"We have residents whose homes flood three, four times a year," May said during last Thursday's commission meeting. "At what point does saving for a rainy day become absurd when it's already raining?"

The immediate question before commissioners was narrow: whether to allocate $8.5 million from reserves to accelerate drainage improvements in the Brent, Ensley and Brownsville areas before hurricane season begins June 1. The vote failed 3-2, with Kohler, Chairman Jeff Bergosh and Commissioner Steven Barry opposed.

But the 47-minute discussion that preceded that vote revealed fractures in how the board thinks about money, risk and the basic purpose of local government.

The math behind the mattress

County policy requires maintaining reserves equal to at least two months of operating expenditures—roughly $43 million based on the current $258 million general fund budget. The $47.2 million balance exceeds that threshold by about $4 million.

That cushion has grown steadily since 2020, when federal COVID relief funds covered expenses that would otherwise have drawn down reserves. Property tax revenues have also outpaced projections for three consecutive years as home values climbed.

Kohler, who chairs the board's budget committee, calls the current reserve level "barely adequate" given the county's hurricane exposure.

"FEMA reimbursement takes years," he said. "After Ivan, after Dennis, after Sally—we know this. You need cash on hand to respond, to rebuild, to keep services running while you wait for federal money that may or may not come."

He's not wrong about the timeline. Escambia County is still resolving FEMA claims from Hurricane Sally in 2020. Some Ivan-related reimbursements weren't finalized until 2012, eight years after the storm.

But May and Commissioner Damond Walters argue that logic creates a self-defeating cycle. If reserves can never be touched because a hurricane might come, they're not really reserves at all. They're a monument to inaction.

"We budget for emergencies, then treat the emergency fund as untouchable," Walters said. "Meanwhile, actual emergencies are happening every week in neighborhoods where the drainage hasn't been upgraded since the 1970s."

Drainage as disaster prevention

The proposed $8.5 million would have funded improvements to three drainage basins that county engineers have flagged as critical. Combined, they affect approximately 4,200 residential properties.

The Brent Lane basin near Highway 29 floods during any rainfall exceeding two inches per hour. The Fairfield Drive system in Ensley backs up into homes during sustained rain. The Brownsville area experiences regular street flooding that has damaged vehicles and made evacuation routes impassable during past storms.

County Public Works Director Joy Jones told commissioners the projects could be completed by early fall if funding were approved immediately. Delaying until the next budget cycle, which won't be finalized until September, pushes construction into late 2026 or 2027.

"The engineering is done," Jones said. "We're shovel-ready. What we lack is authorization to spend."

Barry, who represents the district containing two of the three affected areas, nonetheless voted against the allocation. His reasoning: the county should address drainage comprehensively through the upcoming budget process rather than piecemeal through reserve draws.

"I want these projects funded," Barry said. "I don't want to set a precedent where every commissioner comes to the board asking for reserve money for their district's priorities."

Two philosophies, one budget

The exchange points to a tension that will define budget season. Kohler and Bergosh favor what Bergosh calls "fiscal conservatism"—maintaining high reserves, limiting new recurring expenditures, prioritizing debt reduction. They've successfully pushed to pay down the county's pension liability ahead of schedule and have resisted creating new staff positions.

May and Walters represent a different view. They argue the county has systematically underinvested in infrastructure serving lower-income and minority communities. They point to capital improvement plans that repeatedly defer projects in their districts while funding expansions elsewhere.

"Conservatism for whom?" May asked. "We found money for the soccer complex. We found money for the downtown parking garage. We find money when the priority is there. Reserves become the excuse when it isn't."

Barry occupies the middle ground—fiscally cautious but responsive to constituent pressure. His vote often decides close questions. On the drainage issue, he sided with delay. On other matters, he's broken with Kohler and Bergosh.

The dynamic means neither faction can reliably count to three. It also means the upcoming budget negotiations will be unusually contentious.

What hurricane season changes

County Administrator Wes Moreno has proposed a $271 million general fund budget for fiscal year 2027, a 5% increase driven largely by employee compensation and insurance costs. The proposal maintains reserves at current levels while funding several deferred capital projects—though not the drainage improvements May sought.

Moreno's budget assumes no major hurricane. That assumption is standard practice but increasingly uncomfortable as climate data shows intensifying storms and a coastline more developed than ever.

If a significant storm hits before September, the reserve debate becomes moot. The county will spend what it needs to spend. If the season passes quietly, commissioners will face the same argument next spring.

"The fundamental question hasn't changed in 20 years," said Amy Bisceglia, a former county budget analyst now with the University of West Florida's Haas Center. "How much is enough? There's no formula that answers it. It's a political judgment."

That judgment will play out over the next five months as commissioners negotiate the FY2027 budget. Public hearings are scheduled for July and September. The final vote is set for September 17.

In the meantime, the $47.2 million sits in its spreadsheet cell. Safe. Unused. Waiting for a storm that may or may not come.

articles.label: Feature
articles.headline: The last fish house on Joe Patti's block
articles.dek: As downtown Pensacola's waterfront transforms, one family-owned seafood wholesaler holds out against rising property values and redevelopment pressure.
brief: Martinez Seafood Wholesale has operated on West Main Street since 1962, making it one of the oldest continuous businesses in Pensacola's historic fish house district.
The family has rejected three purchase offers in the past 18 months, including one exceeding $2.8 million for their quarter-acre lot.
Rising property assessments and changing zoning regulations threaten the economic viability of the last working fish house on the block.
articles.body:
The ice machine kicks on at 3:47 a.m., same as it has for decades. Rafael Martinez is already elbow-deep in a cooler of gulf shrimp, sorting by size under fluorescent lights that buzz and flicker. Behind him, the concrete loading dock slopes toward Pensacola Bay, slick with fish scales and morning dew.

Across the street, a construction fence surrounds what used to be Hendrix Oyster Company. Before that, it was the lot where shrimpers would drink beer and play dominoes between runs. Now a sign promises "The Wharf at Pensacola: Luxury Waterfront Living Coming 2027."

"They'll be smelling my fish at 4 in the morning," Martinez said, not looking up from his work. "We'll see how luxury that is."

A shrinking district

The 600 block of West Main Street once held seven working fish houses. Wholesalers, ice plants, net repair shops. The kind of businesses that made Pensacola smell like Pensacola. Today, Martinez Seafood Wholesale stands alone among them—a corrugated metal rectangle wedged between a surface parking lot and a cleared foundation.

Joe Patti's Seafood, the famous retail operation one block east, still draws tourists and locals by the hundreds. But the working waterfront that supplied it has mostly vanished. The shrimp boats that once lined the seawall now dock farther west, near the port facilities, or don't come to Pensacola at all.

"People think Joe Patti's is the fish business here," said Carmen Martinez, Rafael's sister and the company's business manager. "They don't know we've been selling to restaurants from Mobile to Tallahassee since Kennedy was president."

Their father, Aurelio Martinez, started the business in 1962 with a pickup truck and a handshake deal with a Bayou La Batre boat captain. The current building went up in 1978. The family owns it outright—no mortgage, no partners.

That ownership is precisely why developers keep calling.

The math of staying

Escambia County property records show the Martinez parcel's assessed value jumped from $412,000 in 2021 to $1.89 million in 2025. The increase reflects the area's transformation from industrial waterfront to what county planners call "mixed-use destination development."

Carmen Martinez produced a property tax bill showing an annual payment of $37,400—up from $8,100 five years ago.

"That's not a fish house tax bill," she said. "That's a condo developer tax bill."

The family's wholesale business generates annual revenue between $600,000 and $800,000, according to Carmen Martinez. Margins are thin. Diesel costs eat into deliveries. The boats they buy from increasingly steam to Louisiana or Alabama, where fuel is cheaper and ice is closer to the fishing grounds.

Three purchase offers have arrived since October 2024. The most recent, from Emerald Coast Development Partners LLC, exceeded $2.8 million—roughly $11 million per acre in a neighborhood where waterfront residential lots now trade above $3 million for a quarter-acre.

The family declined all three.

"My father built this," Rafael Martinez said during a break in the sorting work. "What am I supposed to do, take the money and go work at Publix?"

Zoning as pressure

The Pensacola City Council rezoned much of the West Main Street corridor in 2023, changing designations from Industrial Waterfront to Mixed-Use Waterfront. The change allows residential construction up to six stories and eliminates protections that once prioritized working maritime businesses.

City planning documents from that period describe the goal as "activating underutilized waterfront parcels for community benefit and economic development."

The Martinez property is now a legal nonconforming use. The fish house can continue operating, but any significant modification—expanding the cooler space, rebuilding after storm damage, adding capacity—would trigger a review that could force compliance with new zoning standards.

"They didn't outlaw us," Carmen Martinez said. "They just made it so we can't grow. And eventually, something breaks, and then what?"

District 6 City Council member Alicia Webb, whose district includes the waterfront, said the 2023 rezoning balanced multiple interests.

"We value our maritime heritage," Webb said in a phone interview. "But we also have a responsibility to pursue development that serves the broader tax base. These aren't easy decisions."

Webb declined to comment on the Martinez family's specific situation, citing potential future land-use matters that could come before the council.

What remains

At 6 a.m., a refrigerated truck from a Destin restaurant idles in the Martinez lot. The driver loads twelve cases of red snapper fillets, weighing each on a scale that looks older than he is. The transaction takes eight minutes. No paperwork beyond a handwritten receipt.

This is what remains of Pensacola's fish house economy—a handful of wholesale operations scattered from Joe Patti's to the commercial port, serving a restaurant industry that increasingly sources from national distributors with standardized products and predictable pricing.

"The chefs who care, they find us," Rafael Martinez said. "The chains, they don't need us. They need the same fish every time, from a warehouse in Jacksonville."

He gestured toward the bay, where the morning sun was catching the windows of the high-rise condominiums on Pensacola Beach.

"Look at that," he said. "Beautiful, right? My grandfather fished those waters. Hauled mullet with a cast net, sold it off the back of his boat. Right there. Now it's million-dollar views."

He turned back to his cooler, sorting the next batch.

"We're not against anybody making money," he said. "We just want to keep making ours."

No clear end

The Martinez family has not set a price at which they would sell. They have not discussed retirement timelines. Carmen Martinez is 54, Rafael is 58. Neither has children interested in the business.

"We don't have a plan for that," Carmen Martinez admitted. "Maybe that's stupid. But right now, we have work to do."

Outside, the construction crane across the street began its morning pivot, swinging steel toward the bay. The ice machine hummed. The smell of gulf shrimp hung in the salt air.

For now, the last fish house on the block keeps its lights on.

articles.label: Local News
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings 150,000 visitors—and a city stretched thin
articles.dek: Pensacola Beach Airshow returns July 10-11. City officials say they're ready. Traffic studies and hotel booking data suggest otherwise.
brief: Hotel occupancy across Pensacola Beach and Gulf Breeze has hit 94% for July 10-11 weekend, with average nightly rates exceeding $400
2024 airshow traffic study showed Bob Sikes Bridge backups exceeding 3 hours; no infrastructure changes have been implemented since
Escambia County EMS requested 40% staffing increase for airshow weekend but received approval for only 15%
articles.body:
The parking lot at Casino Beach fills by 6:47 a.m. on airshow Saturday. This is not a projection. This is what happened in 2024, and in 2023, and in every year the Blue Angels have flown their homecoming show over Santa Rosa Island.

By 8 a.m., the Bob Sikes Bridge becomes a parking lot itself. By 10 a.m., families who left downtown Pensacola expecting a 15-minute drive are still sitting in traffic on Gulf Breeze Parkway, watching the jets streak overhead through their windshields.

The 2026 Pensacola Beach Airshow is scheduled for July 10-11. Visit Pensacola estimates 150,000 spectators will descend on the island over the two-day event. City and county officials held a joint press conference last week declaring their readiness. The data tells a different story.

The bridge problem nobody has solved

A traffic impact study commissioned by Santa Rosa Island Authority after the 2024 airshow documented average delays of 2 hours and 47 minutes for vehicles crossing the Bob Sikes Bridge between 7 a.m. and noon on show Saturday. Peak delays exceeded 3 hours and 20 minutes.

The study recommended three interventions: expanded park-and-ride service from the Pensacola Bay Center, temporary traffic signal timing modifications on Gulf Breeze Parkway, and a hard vehicle cap for the island with real-time monitoring.

None of these have been implemented. None are currently funded.

"We're working with our regional partners on traffic flow optimization," said Keith Wilkins, public information officer for Escambia County. When asked which specific measures would be in place for July, Wilkins said details would be released "closer to the event."

Santa Rosa Island Authority did not respond to three requests for comment over the past two weeks.

Hotels are booked. Emergency services are not.

Booking data from Visit Pensacola shows hotel occupancy for the July 10-11 weekend at 94% across Pensacola Beach and Gulf Breeze properties. The average nightly rate has climbed to $412—up from $287 during the 2024 airshow weekend. Vacation rental platforms show similar saturation, with beachfront properties on Via de Luna commanding $800 or more per night.

The economic impact is substantial. Visit Pensacola projects $23 million in direct visitor spending over the four-day period surrounding the airshow.

What that spending buys in emergency response capacity is less clear.

Documents obtained through a public records request show Escambia County EMS submitted a staffing proposal in February requesting a 40% increase in on-duty personnel for airshow weekend. The proposal cited heat-related illness data from previous years—37 patients transported in 2024, 44 in 2023—and noted that island access delays compromise response times for the entire southern portion of the county.

The County Administrator's office approved a 15% staffing increase. The gap, according to the proposal's own projections, leaves response capacity below recommended levels if patient volume matches 2023 figures.

"We'll have adequate coverage," said Wilkins. He declined to provide specific staffing numbers.

What 150,000 people look like

The population of Pensacola Beach on a typical summer Saturday is roughly 25,000, counting residents, hotel guests, and day visitors. The airshow multiplies that by six.

The island has one grocery store. One urgent care clinic, which closes at 5 p.m. Two gas stations, both of which ran dry before noon during the 2023 show.

The Portofino towers can hold 1,200 guests. The Hampton Inn, 120. The Holiday Inn, 150. Add the smaller properties and vacation rentals, and the island's fixed-roof capacity sits around 8,000 overnight visitors. The rest of the crowd—the vast majority—drives in, parks where they can, and leaves when the show ends.

All of them cross the same bridge.

The Navy's role

Naval Air Station Pensacola, home base for the Blue Angels, handles its own traffic for the show. The base opens its gates to spectators with military ID and their guests, absorbing perhaps 15,000 of the total crowd. NAS Pensacola security forces manage on-base logistics independently.

The Navy does not contribute funding or personnel to off-base traffic management. A NAS Pensacola spokesperson confirmed the base coordinates with county emergency services but declined to detail the nature of that coordination.

"We're focused on delivering a safe and spectacular show for our community," the spokesperson said.

What happens next

Escambia County Commission will hear a presentation on airshow logistics at its May 7 meeting. The agenda item, listed as "Special Events Coordination Update," carries no action items and no funding requests.

Santa Rosa Island Authority meets April 24. Airshow planning does not appear on the published agenda.

The Blue Angels will fly regardless. They've flown the Pensacola Beach homecoming since 1946, with only pandemic-related interruptions. The jets will trace their diamond formation over the emerald water, and the crowd will crane their necks skyward, and for those hours the logistics will not matter.

The logistics will matter on the drive home. They'll matter in the emergency room if the heat index hits 105. They'll matter to the family stuck on the bridge when their toddler needs a bathroom that doesn't exist.

Eighty-three days until showtime. The clock is running.
